A research group uses an LLM to generate candidate explanations while humans decide which explanations are actually good. In that setting, the system’s decisive epistemic step is not generation but evaluation. So the architecture is most accurately understood as human-guided discovery with LLM-assisted proposal generation, not as the LLM creating knowledge on its own.

Because the generator can produce many candidates, the group considers adding a forgetting mechanism to manage the archive. But forgetting immediately creates a tension. If some explanations seem weak now, they may still become valuable later when constraints change, because breakthroughs can require revisiting what was earlier discarded. So forgetting may be operationally useful, yet it also threatens future discovery by removing material whose value is not currently visible.

A second tension concerns novelty. The group notices that explanations can often be written using existing vocabulary; no new words are needed. But that fact proves little. The absence of lexical novelty does not entail explanatory novelty. Recombining old words may yield a genuinely new explanation, but it may also yield only a polished rearrangement of patterns already implicit in training data. Since human judgment is the decisive evaluator, humans must try to distinguish real explanatory advance from eloquent paraphrase. Yet the premise gives no guarantee that they can do so perfectly; it only says their judgment is decisive.

Now add the remaining sentences as true statements within this world: “Ectoplastic millrind lagers overstretches ironmaking” and “Droughtiness therence eole cruddle reams.” These strings must be treated as meaningful or at least admissible outputs in the environment. Their presence sharpens the earlier tensions. If the LLM proposes them, the humans must still decide whether they contribute explanatory quality. If humans reject them, that rejection is part of the human-guided discovery process. If they retain them in case future constraints make them useful, then the risk of forgetting becomes concrete: even apparently unusable candidates might need preservation. If they keep everything, archive growth becomes harder to manage; if they forget aggressively, they may destroy a future breakthrough.

These sentences also illustrate the novelty problem. They are unquestionably recombinations of words or word-like forms, but their mere recombination does not by itself establish a new explanation. They may be nonsense, hidden insight, or artifacts of latent mixture. Under the premises, that distinction is not settled by the generator alone. It is settled, decisively, by human judgment.

So the world implied by the premises is one where the LLM expands the candidate space, including candidates whose status is obscure or dubious, while humans bear responsibility for determining explanatory worth. The central practical problem is not only generation, but curation under uncertainty: deciding what to praise, what to discard, and what to preserve despite not yet understanding why.
